plainMeaning: This protection is hugely powerful, destroys petty troubles, springs from the lines of the Navkāra mantra, and was handed down by the teachers of old.
recitation: mahāprabhāvā rakṣeyaṁ, kṣudropadrava-nāśinī | parameṣṭhi-padodbhūtā, kathitā pūrva-sūribhiḥ ||

wordByWord.etymology: Compound of mahā ('great') + prabhāva ('majestic power, efficacy, potency'); feminine nominative, qualifying rakṣā.
wordByWord.notes: prabhāva names the efficacious 'charge' of a mantra/practice, not mere strength.

wordByWord.etymology: Sandhi of rakṣā ('guarding, protection'; from √rakṣ, 'to guard') + iyaṁ ('this'): rakṣā + iyaṁ → rakṣeyaṁ. Feminine nominative.
wordByWord.notes: The grammatical subject of the verse; all four epithets agree with it in the feminine.

wordByWord.etymology: kṣudra ('petty, small, minor') + upadrava ('affliction, calamity, disturbance, visitation'); first member of the compound kṣudropadrava-nāśinī.
wordByWord.notes: kṣudra is deliberate: the worldly, lesser disturbances, not the deep karmic bondage.

wordByWord.etymology: Feminine agent-noun from √naś ('to perish'), causative sense 'causing to perish'; nāśin + ī. Cf. jñāna-vairāgya-nāśinī, 'destroyer of wisdom and detachment'.
wordByWord.notes: Completes the compound 'destroyer of petty afflictions'.

wordByWord.etymology: First member of the compound parameṣṭhi-padodbhūtā; from parama ('highest') + √sthā ('to stand') — those established at the summit.
wordByWord.notes: Printed as a separate word but bound to padodbhūtā as one compound.

wordByWord.etymology: pada ('word, line, step, foot') + udbhūtā ('arisen, sprung up', past participle of ud-√bhū). Feminine nominative.
wordByWord.notes: pada here = the nine lines (padas) of the Navkāra; the source/authority of the rakṣā.

wordByWord.etymology: Feminine past passive participle of √kath ('to tell, narrate, declare'); kathita + ā.
wordByWord.notes: Passive: the rakṣā 'was taught', completing the eulogy.

wordByWord.gloss: by the former preceptors (the earlier ācāryas)
wordByWord.etymology: pūrva ('former, earlier') + sūri ('learned preceptor, wise teacher, ācārya'); instrumental plural -bhiḥ ('by').
wordByWord.notes: Instrumental of agent with the passive kathitā.

literalTranslation: Of great power [is] this protection, destroyer of petty afflictions; sprung from the Parameṣṭhi-padas, [it was] told by the former preceptors.
idiomaticTranslation: Mighty in its power, this guarding spell shatters every petty calamity; risen from the very lines that salute the Supreme Beings, it was declared of old by the preceptors who went before.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: With the fortress fully sealed in v6, the poet now steps out of the second-person visualization and turns to praise the rakṣā itself in the third person — a transition that readies the reciter for the explicit reward-statement to come. Four feminine epithets, all governed by the sandhi-formed subject rakṣeyaṁ ('this protection', i.e. rakṣā iyaṁ), build a compact eulogy along four axes. (1) Its potency: mahāprabhāvā — not brute force but mantric efficacy. (2) Its function: kṣudropadrava-nāśinī, 'destroyer of petty afflictions'. The qualifier kṣudra is precise and honest — the armour is claimed to dispel the lesser, worldly disturbances (illness, fear, obstacles), the very upadravas the closing verse will name, rather than overreaching to promise liberation outright. (3) Its source: parameṣṭhi-padodbhūtā — the protection 'springs from the padas', the salutation-lines just mapped onto the body; its power is borrowed from the Five Supreme Beings, not from the syllables as autonomous magic. (4) Its pedigree: kathitā pūrvasūribhiḥ — 'told by the former preceptors', anchoring the practice in received tradition rather than personal invention. The verse thus answers, in one breath, why the cage holds: it is powerful, it works against real troubles, it derives from the holiest formula, and it carries the warrant of the ancients.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes two characteristically Jain commitments. First, the word kṣudra calibrates the claim doctrinally: namaskāra-bhakti yields supreme spiritual fruit, but its advertised worldly benefit here is modest and bounded — the removal of minor afflictions — keeping devotional protection distinct from the soul's deeper liberative work. Second, padodbhūtā affirms that the efficacy resides in the Parameṣṭhi-padas themselves: reverence to the arihanta–siddha–ācārya–upādhyāya–sādhu is the wellspring of all auspiciousness, so the guarding power is a derived radiance of that salutation. kathitā pūrvasūribhiḥ supplies the epistemic warrant — authority by guru-paramparā, the transmitted testimony of qualified teachers, rather than self-assertion.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: When reciting this verse the practitioner shifts from building the image to trusting it: the line teaches that the safety just visualized rests on the Parameṣṭhins and on a verified lineage, not on the reciter's own contrivance — so it is to be received with śraddhā (faith). It also sets realistic expectation: this is a shield against everyday troubles, fortifying calm before the reward is spelt out.

crossReferences.text: Pañca-Namaskāra (Navkāra) mantra — the nine padas named as the source (parameṣṭhi-padodbhūtā)
Following verse (v8) phalaśruti: 'tasya na syād bhayaṁ vyādhi-rādhiś-cāpi kadācana' — the petty upadravas this verse promises to destroy

sources.title: Upadrava — definition (Sanskrit/Jain context)
sources.consultedFor: Sense of upadrava as 'affliction, calamity, disturbance' for kṣudropadrava.

sources.title: Nashini (Nāśinī) — definition
sources.consultedFor: Feminine agent-noun 'she who destroys' (√naś), confirming the nāśinī form and compound usage.

sources.title: Parameshthin (Parameṣṭhi) — definitions incl. Jainism
sources.consultedFor: Confirming the Jain Parameṣṭhin sense underlying parameṣṭhi-padodbhūtā.

sources.title: Suri (Sūri) — definitions incl. Jainism/Prakrit
sources.consultedFor: Sense of sūri as 'learned preceptor/ācārya' for pūrvasūribhiḥ.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim verse text and the 'pūrva-sūris' attribution.

notes: rakṣeyaṁ is read as rakṣā + iyaṁ ('this protection') by ā+i→e sandhi; an alternative parsing as rakṣā eṣā is possible but yields the same sense. parameṣṭhi and padodbhūtā are printed as two words yet form a single compound (parameṣṭhi-padodbhūtā). kṣudra is read with deliberate force ('petty/minor afflictions'), bounding the claim to worldly disturbances. The book's orthography, the daṇḍa '।' after pāda 2, and the final period are preserved verbatim. The named agents are the unspecified 'former preceptors' (pūrva-sūris), consistent with the preface's stance that no single composer is attested.
